items.q: What defines a star as being on the 'main sequence'?
items.correct: It fuses hydrogen into helium in its core.
items.misconception: It is the largest stage of a star's life.
items.why: Students think main sequence = most spectacular. It's defined by the FUEL: hydrogen fusion. Stars spend most of their lives there because hydrogen is plentiful and fusion is stable.

items.q: What is the principle behind stellar parallax?
items.correct: A nearby star appears to shift against background stars as Earth orbits the Sun.
items.misconception: Stars actually move position throughout the year.
items.why: Students think stars literally move. Parallax is an APPARENT shift due to our changing viewpoint - same reason your finger seems to jump when you alternate eyes. The closer the star, the bigger the shift.

items.q: What is the current best estimate for the age of the universe?
items.why: Students may hold non-scientific views or guess. The current consensus from CMB measurements and stellar ages is approximately 13.8 billion years - the time since the Big Bang.

items.q: Which coordinate system is based on the horizon?
items.why: It's Horizontal. “Equatorial” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What units is right ascension measured in?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Hours. “Degrees” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: If your latitude is 52 N, what is the altitude of Polaris in degrees?
items.why: Exact answer: 52. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
